name: Osprey Acquisition Corp. III
business: Osprey Acquisition Corp. III is a blank check company formed to effect a business combination, targeting companies in the energy industry. The company seeks to acquire businesses deploying disruptive technologies and next-generation infrastructure that modernize energy systems, enable AI-driven optimization, and support global connectivity.

exposures.sentence: Specifically, we seek companies deploying disruptive technologies and next-generation infrastructure that modernize energy systems, enable AI-driven optimization, and support the resilient, sustainable backbone of global connectivity.
Energy Infrastructure Platforms: Transmission, distribution, and grid modernization assets supporting electrification, rising electricity demand, and system reliability.

Utility-Scale Battery Storage & Virtual Power Plants (VPPs): Battery storage systems, distributed energy resource aggregation platforms, and software-enabled grid services that help balance supply and demand across increasingly complex power systems.

Next-Generation Nuclear & Firm Power: Small modular reactors, advanced nuclear technologies, and other dispatchable power generation assets that may support reliable baseload electricity supply in an evolving power mix.
Traditional Oil & Gas Platforms: Oil and natural gas production, mineral and royalty ownership, LNG infrastructure, and midstream systems enabling reliable and affordable energy supply
AI-Enabled Energy & Grid Optimization: Software, analytics, and digital control systems improving reliability, forecasting, and asset performance across energy systems.

Critical Materials & Energy Supply Chains: Processing, recycling, and strategic inputs required across the energy value chain, including materials used in electrification technologies, batteries, and energy infrastructure.

Electricity demand in the United States is also projected to increase meaningfully over the remainder of the decade, driven by emerging load categories including artificial intelligence infrastructure, cloud computing data centers, transportation electrification, and industrial activity.

tamClaims.claim: Global energy investment reached approximately $3.3 trillion in 2025.
tamClaims.sentence: According to the International Energy Agency’s World Energy Investment 2025 report, global energy investment reached approximately $3.3 trillion in 2025, including approximately $1.1 trillion invested in fossil fuel supply and approximately $2.2 trillion invested across other energy systems such as renewables, nuclear, electricity networks, storage, low-emissions fuels, efficiency technologies, and electrification initiatives.

tamClaims.claim: Data centers could increase from 2.5% to 9% of U.S. electricity consumption by 2030.
tamClaims.sentence: For example, the Electric Power Research Institute’s Powering Intelligence (2024) report estimates that data centers represented approximately 2.5% of total U.S. electricity consumption in 2022 and could increase to approximately 9% by 2030.

tamClaims.claim: Global grid investment may need to exceed $600 billion annually by early 2030s.
tamClaims.sentence: The International Energy Agency estimates that global grid investment may need to exceed approximately $600 billion annually by the early 2030s to support rising electricity demand and integrate new generation resources.

tamClaims.claim: U.S. power grid interconnection queue exceeded 2,600 GW of proposed capacity as of 2024.
tamClaims.sentence: According to the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, the U.S. power grid interconnection queue exceeded approximately 2,600 gigawatts of proposed generation capacity as of 2024, compared to roughly 1,200 gigawatts of total installed generation capacity in the United States, highlighting the scale of proposed development and the complexity of bringing new projects online.

tamClaims.claim: Global battery storage capacity could increase more than sixfold by 2030.
tamClaims.sentence: BloombergNEF estimates that global battery storage capacity could increase more than sixfold by 2030 as utilities and grid operators deploy storage technologies to improve grid flexibility and integrate variable generation resources.
